en_title: The Geography Of Vietnam's Three Regions: Mountains
en_body:
North Vietnam: North Vietnam has more mountains than plains; the central delta is ancient sea gradually filled by alluvium. The mountains are low. Fang-Di-Păng (3,142 m) belongs to the Hoàng-Liên-Sơn range. In the highlands, beyond Lạng-Sơn, there is Tam-Thanh grotto with the Vọng-Phu stone; the Kỳ-Cùng River; above all, the Perfume Pagoda Grotto in Yến-Vi village, Mỹ Đức prefecture, Hà-Đông province; and the Đáy River: yet these wonders hold so many legends and poetic ancient histories. Many poets of old came to visit and left behind very beautiful poems,

 Behold mountains mountains waters waters clouds clouds
 Is the First Grotto truly here?
 Softly in the apricot woods, birds offer fruit,
 Dreamily in the stream, fish listen to sutras.
 (Chu Mạnh Trinh)

Phú-Thọ has two celebrated scenic places: Nghĩa-Lĩnh mountain, the sacred ground where the temple of the ancestral Hùng Kings stands. Ninh-Bình has Dục-Thúy mountain and Non-Nước Pagoda, together with Cánh-Diêu and Hồi-Hạc mountains. There are also Yên-Tử mountain in Đông-Triều; Cánh-Phượng mountain in Sơn-Tây; Chè mountain in Bắc-Ninh.

Central Vietnam: Central Vietnam lies like the rib-bone of Vietnam, renowned for the Trường-Sơn range running the whole length of the poorest homeland of the three regions: 120 km at its widest, and at its narrowest in Quảng-Trị only 40 km. Forming a carpenter's square with the Trường-Sơn range are the lesser mountain ranges near Mũi Chân-Mây and Ải-Vân Pass.
 Southwest of Central Vietnam are immense plateaus including Ngọc-Ang with its highest granite summit (2,398 m); Pleiku bears traces of volcanoes. Darlac is the broadest, 100 square km; Lâm-Viên holds Đà-Lạt, the tourist city renowned throughout the world before 30-4-1975; Lâm-Viên also has two other broad plateaus, Di-Linh and M'Nong.

South Vietnam: This highland region descends into South Vietnam through Bà Đen mountain in Tây-Ninh and Chứa-Chan mountain (Long-Khánh). Quảng-Nam is renowned for the Ngũ-Hành-Sơn range with Tàng-Chân grotto. In Quảng-Ngãi the mountains are high and perilous, the forests immense and dense: highest are Trà-Bông and Thạch-Bích mountains; lower are Cao-Môn, Giản, Tham-Hội, Phụng-Hoàng, Phố-Tiên, Ình-Ình.

 Yet Thất-Sơn in An-Giang is the most renowned mountain region of South Vietnam. In Châu-Đốc is Sam mountain, lying 5 km west of the provincial capital: Tâm-Am-Tự stands at the mountain's foot. Two sacred landmarks must be named: the tomb of Thoại-Ngọc-Hầu and the Temple of Bà Chúa Xứ. Beyond Sam mountain and Tịnh-Biên district is, naturally: the scenic realm of Hà-Tiên: and most famous in this land is the daughter of Hà-Tiên.
note: The quoted quatrain is by Chu Mạnh Trinh, and “the daughter of Hà-Tiên” alludes to the celebrated women associated with Hà-Tiên's literary and cultural memory.
